title: Core Primitives Barrel
description: Start where every package starts: the @rtcforge/core public entry point, the single most depended-upon file in the codebase (fan-in 31). It re-exports the shared building blocks every other package imports — EventEmitter, errors, Clock, IdGenerator, StateStore, MessageBus, Lock, HashRing, Membership, and Gossip. As a consumer you rarely import core directly, but understanding these primitives explains the vocabulary used across the signaling, sdk, media, and sfu APIs you will actually call.
languageLesson: A TypeScript barrel file (index.ts that only re-exports with 'export *' / 'export { ... }') gives a package one clean import surface. Consumers write `import { EventEmitter } from '@rtcforge/core'` without knowing the internal file layout, and the maintainer can move files freely as long as the barrel stays stable.

title: Eventing and Errors
description: Two core primitives surface in nearly every public API, so learn them before the higher-level packages. EventEmitter is the typed pub/sub base that RTCForgeClient, Call, and the signaling server all extend so you can subscribe to events like connection state and track changes. The errors module defines the typed error classes thrown across packages, so your try/catch and error handlers can branch on a known type rather than parsing strings.

title: Signaling Server: Entry Surface
description: Every call needs a signaling server to broker who-is-in-which-room and to relay SDP/ICE between peers. The @rtcforge/signaling barrel exposes that surface, and SignalingServer is its façade — the highest fan-out file in the package (11), wiring together rooms, peers, auth, rate limiting, and heartbeats. As a consumer this is the first thing you instantiate on your backend: you give it an HTTP/WebSocket server and it becomes the rendezvous point your browser clients connect to.

title: Rooms, Peers, and the Wire Protocol
description: Inside the signaling server, a Room groups the Peers currently in a call and a Peer represents one connected client (both high fan-out coordinators). The protocol and types modules define the message envelopes exchanged over the socket — join, offer, answer, ice-candidate — which are the contract your client and server must agree on. Building on the EventEmitter base from Step 2, Room and Peer emit lifecycle events (peer joined, peer left) you can hook into for presence and UI.

title: Securing the Server: Auth & Rate Limiting
description: Before exposing signaling to the public internet you need to gate it. The Authenticator validates client credentials/tokens at join time, the RateLimiter throttles abusive senders, and the HeartbeatMonitor detects and prunes dead connections so stale peers do not linger in rooms. These are the production hardening knobs you configure when constructing the SignalingServer from Step 3.

title: Client SDK: RTCForgeClient
description: Now switch to the browser side. The @rtcforge/sdk barrel exposes RTCForgeClient, the main object your front-end code creates to connect to the signaling server from Step 3 (fan-out 9 — it orchestrates transport, reconnect, queuing, and rooms). This is the second half of the wiring: server in Steps 3-5, client here. You construct a client, point it at the server URL, and join a room.

title: Transport & Resilient Delivery
description: Underneath the client sits a swappable Transport abstraction (Transport.ts plus its types form a tightly coupled pair the analysis flagged as a cluster), with WebSocketTransport as the default browser implementation. ReconnectStrategy applies exponential backoff on drops and SendQueue buffers outbound messages while disconnected, so a flaky network does not lose signaling messages. As a consumer you mostly get this for free, but knowing the seam lets you supply a custom transport or tune reconnect behavior.
languageLesson: Transport is defined as a TypeScript interface, and WebSocketTransport is one concrete implementation. Programming the client against the interface (dependency inversion) is what lets you swap in a different wire — a test double, a long-poll fallback — without touching RTCForgeClient.

title: Building a P2P Call (Mesh)
description: With signaling and a connected client in place, @rtcforge/media is how you actually move audio and video. Call is the client-side mesh orchestrator (fan-out 9, the hub of the densest cluster in the graph): it creates one PeerConnection per remote peer, runs perfect-negotiation, and manages add/replace/remove of tracks and mute. This is the payoff step — signaling + sdk + media combine here to produce a working multi-party call.
languageLesson: WebRTC 'perfect negotiation' is a pattern that assigns each peer a polite/impolite role so simultaneous offers do not deadlock. The Call orchestrator implements it so you never hand-manage offer/answer glare — you just add a track and it renegotiates.

title: Tracks, Capture, and Active Speaker
description: Rounding out the mesh call: LocalTrackRegistry tracks the local camera/mic tracks you publish, the browser entry point provides capture helpers for getUserMedia and screen-share, and ActiveSpeakerDetector polls WebRTC getStats audio levels to tell you who is currently talking (handy for speaker-view UIs). These plug into the Call from Step 8 via its onChange-style callbacks.

title: From Mesh to SFU: Server-Side Media
description: Mesh calls do not scale past a handful of peers because every client uploads to every other client. The SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit) path moves forwarding to the server: MediaService is the server-side façade, MediaRouter routes media between Producers (upstream tracks) and Consumers (downstream subscriptions), and WorkerPool spreads the CPU-heavy forwarding across worker processes. Producer and Consumer both extend MediaEntity, the shared base for routable media objects.

title: Scaling Out: SFU Cluster
description: @rtcforge/sfu takes the single-server SFU and scales it horizontally across many nodes. The barrel exposes SfuCluster (the cluster coordinator) and SfuNode (a single server's identity in the cluster, the highest fan-in type in this package after its shared types). CascadingRouter and CascadeTree plan a tree of SFU nodes so a large room can span multiple servers, each forwarding only to its children. This is how you grow from one box to a fleet.

title: Placement, Bandwidth, and Failure Handling
description: Scaling out demands decisions about which node hosts which room and what happens when a node dies. PlacementStrategy (fan-out 7) decides node assignment — including consistent-hash placement built on core's HashRing — SimpleBandwidthEstimator informs routing under load, and NodeFailureTracker plus HealthChecker detect and react to unhealthy nodes so the cascade tree can heal. These tie back to the core primitives from Steps 1-2.

title: The Gossip Wire: adapter-udp
description: Cluster nodes must agree on membership without a central registry. Core's Gossip primitive implements an epidemic protocol for spreading membership state, and @rtcforge/adapter-udp's UdpGossipTransport is the concrete Node.js datagram wire that carries gossip between SFU nodes. As a consumer scaling to a cluster, this is the pluggable transport you wire into the membership layer — the last seam in the stack, mirroring the swappable Transport pattern from Step 7.

title: Package Wiring & Dependencies
description: Finally, the manifests that tell npm how these packages relate. The root package.json defines the monorepo workspaces and shared tooling, while each package's package.json declares its name, entry points, and inter-package dependencies — for example sdk and media depending on core, and adapter-udp depending on core. Reading these confirms the install order and which packages you actually need to add to your own project versus which come along transitively.
languageLesson: npm workspaces let one repo publish many packages that depend on each other by name. The root package.json lists `workspaces`, and each child's package.json uses `exports`/`main`/`types` fields to declare its public entry points — the same barrels you saw in Steps 1, 3, 6, 11, and 13.
